svg.text: 07 - External Integrations, Caching, and Fallbacks
What this covers: provider inventory, normalization, resilience mechanics, and cost posture.
Integration Goal
Turn heterogeneous third-party APIs into reliable canonical condition signals that Buddi can reason on safely.
Core pattern: provider adapters + rate budgets + circuit breakers + fallback chain + stale-data policy.
Provider Inventory (by domain)
Weather
Primary: OpenWeatherMap One Call 3.0
Fallback: Open-Meteo; Secondary/Marine enrichment: WeatherAPI
Tides
Primary global: WorldTides; NZ high-accuracy supplement: NIWA
Solar/Celestial/Aurora
sunrise-sunset.org, NOAA SWPC (Kp), Astronomy sources, OpenUV
Maps/Routing/POI
Mapbox (primary), OSRM fallback, Overpass OSM primary POI, Google Places enrichment.
Wildlife + local signals
iNaturalist + DOC/MetService/GeoNet style official feeds for domain-critical updates.
Why this mix works
Coverage breadth + free-tier viability at MVP + paid scale path when load increases.
Vendor diversity reduces single-provider outage risk.
Normalization and Canonical Schemas
Adapters map provider-specific fields into canonical models:
WeatherCondition (temp, feels_like, humidity, pressure, wind, precip, UV, visibility, icon)
TideCondition/TideEvent (high/low, heights, station metadata, datum)
SolarCondition (sunrise/sunset, golden hours, moon phase, Kp probability)
Unit standardization examples
m/s->km/h, miles->km, feet->meters, Fahrenheit->Celsius, UTC ISO timestamps.
Forecast confidence by horizon
0-24h high confidence, 24-72h moderate, 4-7 days trend-level only.
This confidence directly tunes swap aggressiveness and phrasing certainty.
Caching tiers
L1 in-memory hot cache, L2 Redis operational cache, L3 Postgres historical archive.
Location rounding improves hit rate while preserving practical spatial accuracy.
Prefetch behavior
Active-trip windows are proactively warmed so briefing generation avoids cold-start latency.
Resilience and Cost Governance
Rate budgets
per-provider minute/day limits in Redis sliding windows + burst allowances.
fallback provider consumption when primary quotas are exhausted.
Circuit breaker states
CLOSED normal, OPEN failover, HALF_OPEN probe recovery after timeout.
Fallback chains (examples)
Weather: OWM -> Open-Meteo -> WeatherAPI -> stale cache (flagged).
Tides: WorldTides -> NIWA (NZ context-specific priority rules) -> stale cache (flagged).
Routing: Mapbox -> OSRM -> straight-line estimate fallback.
Stale-data policy
if all providers fail: serve last good snapshot with explicit stale flag and caveated user messaging.
hard upper age warnings for weather/tide prevent silent bad guidance.
Cost posture
MVP expected near free-tier boundaries; at scale, map/places enrichment can dominate costs.
Integration Implementation Sequence
1) Build canonical schemas and adapter interfaces first.
2) Implement primary providers + robust validation and retries.
3) Layer Redis rate-limiter + circuit-breaker control plane.
4) Add fallback adapters and stale-mode user messaging path.
5) Add prefetch jobs and freshness metrics for operational confidence.
Monitoring Metrics That Matter
provider success rate, breaker state changes, fallback usage %, stale serve rate, cache hit %, normalized-data validation failures.
Business impact metric: missed or wrong condition calls that would have changed trip recommendations.
Engineering Risk Notes
- inconsistent provider timezones can silently break day-level planning if not normalized at ingestion.
- absent schema checks allow malformed fallback responses to poison cache and downstream scoring.
- no cache partitioning by data type/horizon causes accidental over/under freshness semantics.
Next recommended read: 08 - NZ Pilot Domain Intelligence.
